theorem_title: Algebraic tensor relations from Tucker decompositions
theorem_statement: For the family of block tensors constructed from generic input matrices, there is a polynomial map \(F\), independent of the ambient size parameter and of uniformly bounded degree, whose vanishing detects exactly the intended rank-one blockwise scaling relations among the tensors.
raw_proof_text:
We describe the construction of the polynomial map and prove that its vanishing is equivalent to the desired blockwise rank-one scaling condition.

Assemble the given family of tensors into a single block tensor \(T\). The tensor \(T\) should be viewed as a four-way tensor: the four modes record the four tensor directions, while the block indices record which component of the family is being used. Define the polynomial map \(F\) by taking a fixed finite list of minors of the four matrix flattenings of \(T\). Since each minor has bounded size, the coordinate degree of \(F\) is bounded independently of the ambient parameter.

The first claim is that every tensor coming from the intended construction lies in the zero set of \(F\). The reason is that the assembled tensor admits an explicit Tucker decomposition
\[
T=G\times_1 U_1\times_2 U_2\times_3 U_3\times_4 U_4.
\]
Here \(G\) is a fixed core tensor and the \(U_i\) are factor matrices built from the original input matrices. Entrywise, this identity says that
\[
T_{i_1i_2i_3i_4}
=
\sum_{a_1,a_2,a_3,a_4}
G_{a_1a_2a_3a_4}(U_1)_{i_1a_1}(U_2)_{i_2a_2}(U_3)_{i_3a_3}(U_4)_{i_4a_4}.
\]
By construction of \(G\), the only nonzero terms in this sum are precisely the terms corresponding to the allowed permutation pattern. Their signs and coefficients agree with the determinant expansion defining the tensor entries. Hence the Tucker product reproduces exactly the tensor \(T\).

A Tucker decomposition bounds multilinear rank. For example, the mode-1 flattening of \(T\) factors through the columns of \(U_1\), so its rank is at most the number of columns of \(U_1\). The same argument applies to the other modes. Therefore all minors of the flattenings larger than these rank bounds vanish. Since the coordinates of \(F\) are exactly such minors, \(F(T)=0\) for every tensor produced by the intended construction.

Next suppose we scale the blocks of \(T\) by a coefficient tensor \(C\). If \(C\) agrees off the diagonal with a rank-one tensor
\[
C_{i_1i_2i_3i_4}=a_{i_1}b_{i_2}c_{i_3}d_{i_4},
\]
then blockwise multiplication by \(C\) is the same, away from irrelevant diagonal blocks, as multiplying the four tensor modes by diagonal matrices. In other words, the scaled tensor is
\[
T' = T\times_1 D_a\times_2 D_b\times_3 D_c\times_4 D_d,
\]
where \(D_a,D_b,D_c,D_d\) are diagonal matrices. Multiplication by invertible diagonal matrices does not change multilinear rank. Hence all the chosen flattening minors vanish for \(T'\), so \(F(T')=0\).

Conversely, suppose that the chosen minors vanish for a blockwise scaling \(C\). We must show that \(C\) factors as a rank-one product off the diagonal. The proof extracts equations from carefully chosen flattening minors. Because the relevant flattening ranks are bounded, each chosen determinant must vanish. For each chosen submatrix, a direct determinant expansion gives a factorization of the form
\[
\det(M)=A\cdot R(C),
\]
where \(A\) is a polynomial depending only on the generic input matrices and \(R(C)\) is a simple binomial or multiplicative relation among the entries of \(C\).

The scalar factor \(A\) is not the zero polynomial. This is checked by exhibiting a single numerical specialization of the generic matrices for which the corresponding determinant coefficient is nonzero. Hence, for generic input data, \(A\ne 0\), and the vanishing of \(\det(M)\) forces \(R(C)=0\).

The relations \(R(C)=0\) obtained from the first family of minors show that all entries of \(C\) involving two fixed distinguished indices have compatible ratios. The second family of minors propagates these ratios to entries involving exactly one distinguished index. The third family propagates them to all off-diagonal entries. Together the relations imply that there exist scalars \(a_i,b_j,c_k,d_\ell\) such that
\[
C_{ijk\ell}=a_i b_j c_k d_\ell
\]
for every off-diagonal block. Thus \(C\) is rank-one off the diagonal.

We have shown both directions. If the blockwise scaling is rank-one off the diagonal, all chosen minors vanish. If all chosen minors vanish for generic input data, the scaling coefficients must satisfy the rank-one off-diagonal factorization relations. Since the minors used to define \(F\) have bounded size and are chosen uniformly, the polynomial map \(F\) is independent of the ambient parameter and has uniformly bounded degree. This proves the theorem.
